claim: The US health care system can save up to $750 million if 7% of patients waiting for kidney transplants participate in the optimized national kidney paired donation program.

result.claim: The US health care system can save up to $750 million if 7% of patients waiting for kidney transplants participate in the optimized national kidney paired donation program.
result.reasoning: The claim is directly supported by evidence found in paper ID 16737210, "Kidney paired donation and optimizing the use of live donor organs." The abstract of this paper explicitly states: "Even if only 7% of patients awaiting kidney transplantation participated in an optimized national KPD program, the health care system could save as much as $750 million." This proposition (prop_2600) directly matches the claim. While the paper's authors and year are unknown, and it has 0 citations, the information is presented as a conclusion from a model-based study within the abstract. No contradictory evidence was found.

evidence.text: The cost per additional QALY was < pound410 using a prioritisation strategy with estimated glomerular filtration rate.
evidence.source: Assessing the cost effectiveness of using prognostic biomarkers with decision models: case study in prioritising patients waiting for coronary artery surgery

evidence.text: A national optimized matching algorithm would result in 47.7% more transplants compared to the 42.0% resulting from an extension of the currently used first-accept scheme (P<.001).
evidence.source: Kidney paired donation and optimizing the use of live donor organs.

evidence.text: A national optimized matching algorithm would reduce the number of pairs required to travel to 2.9% compared to 18.4% when using the currently used first-accept scheme (P<.001).
evidence.source: Kidney paired donation and optimizing the use of live donor organs.

evidence.text: A prioritisation strategy using a risk score with estimated glomerular filtration rate was the most cost effective strategy.
evidence.source: Assessing the cost effectiveness of using prognostic biomarkers with decision models: case study in prioritising patients waiting for coronary artery surgery

evidence.text: Funding was provided by the Medical Research Council.
evidence.source: Chemotherapy options in elderly and frail patients with metastatic colorectal cancer (MRC FOCUS2): an open-label, randomised factorial trial

evidence.text: The study was conducted on patients undergoing renal transplant.
evidence.source: Induction therapy with autologous mesenchymal stem cells in living-related kidney transplants: a randomized controlled trial.

evidence.text: Prioritisation strategies based on a combination of C reactive protein and estimated glomerular filtration rate is unlikely to be cost effective.
evidence.source: Assessing the cost effectiveness of using prognostic biomarkers with decision models: case study in prioritising patients waiting for coronary artery surgery

evidence.text: Pediatric organ donors in the first two years of life were analyzed.
evidence.source: Early life compartmentalization of human T cell differentiation and regulatory function in mucosal and lymphoid tissues

evidence.text: The study aimed to determine the potential impact of improved matching schemes on the number and quality of transplants achievable with KPD.
evidence.source: Kidney paired donation and optimizing the use of live donor organs.

evidence.text: The patients in the study were members of an integrated health care delivery system in northern California.
evidence.source: Hypoglycemic episodes and risk of dementia in older patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus.

evidence.text: The health care system could save as much as $750 million if only 7% of patients awaiting kidney transplantation participated in an optimized national KPD program.
evidence.source: Kidney paired donation and optimizing the use of live donor organs.

evidence.text: A national optimized matching algorithm would result in 34.9% more grafts surviving at 5 years compared to 28.7% with an extension of the currently used first-accept scheme (P<.001).
evidence.source: Kidney paired donation and optimizing the use of live donor organs.

evidence.text: Severe kidney failure was a main outcome measure.
evidence.source: Diabetes treatments and risk of amputation, blindness, severe kidney failure, hyperglycaemia, and hypoglycaemia: open cohort study in primary care

evidence.text: A national optimized matching algorithm would result in better HLA concordance, with 3.0 mismatched antigens compared to 4.5 mismatched antigens using the currently used first-accept scheme (P<.001).
evidence.source: Kidney paired donation and optimizing the use of live donor organs.

evidence.text: Patient and graft survival at 13 to 30 months was similar in all groups.
evidence.source: Induction therapy with autologous mesenchymal stem cells in living-related kidney transplants: a randomized controlled trial.

evidence.text: Adult organ donors were compared to pediatric organ donors.
evidence.source: Early life compartmentalization of human T cell differentiation and regulatory function in mucosal and lymphoid tissues

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 60-120% higher renal mortality (HR 1.59 [1.27-1.99]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: NETs are deposited in inflamed kidneys.
evidence.source: Netting neutrophils in autoimmune small-vessel vasculitis

evidence.text: Highly sensitized patients would benefit 6-fold from a national optimized scheme (2.3% vs 14.1% successfully matched; P<.001).
evidence.source: Kidney paired donation and optimizing the use of live donor organs.

evidence.text: Kidney paired donation (KPD) offers incompatible donor/recipient pairs the opportunity to match for compatible transplants.
evidence.source: Kidney paired donation and optimizing the use of live donor organs.
